one_line_takeaway: Learning attack-category-specific weightings over entropy/disagreement/Mahalanobis-anomaly signals -- rather than one-size-fits-all adversarial training -- substantially improves NIDS robustness to distribution-shift evasion attacks (+18-30 pp accuracy over PGD-adversarial-training on Injection/Morphing) while showing only marginal gains and even small regressions on gradient-based attacks, and its benefit collapses on higher-dimensional datasets where baseline models are already overwhelmed.
key_finding: Individual detection signals are weak in isolation (per-signal AUCs 0.138-0.549), but the learned weighted combination reaches 97.1% AUC with generic weights (alpha=0.163, beta=0.333, gamma=0.504). Gradient-based attacks are consistently disagreement-dominant (beta 0.434-0.528 across FGSM/PGD-Linf/C&W/SPSA), whereas distribution-shift attacks are heterogeneous: Injection is entropy-dominant (alpha=0.433, AUC=0.896) and Morphing is anomaly-dominant (gamma=0.533, AUC=0.988). Two-stage detection (generic screening + category-level refinement) achieves 0.942 average AUC across all 7 attacks, beating generic-only (0.579, +0.363) and attack-specific-only (0.771, +0.172); PGD-L2 (a weak attack, 16.6% ASR) benefits most (AUC 0.502->0.885, +0.383), and category inference is 100% accurate on the 7 evaluated attacks (paper explicitly cautions against extrapolating this to broader taxonomies). On CSE-CIC-IDS2018, AMDS reaches 84.8% overall accuracy and 71.7% F1 versus the AT baseline's 80.3% accuracy / 62.7% F1 (+4.5 pp accuracy, +9.0 pp F1); AT itself provides essentially no benefit over the Standard ensemble (-0.6 pp accuracy, +0.3 pp F1). Gains are concentrated on distribution-shift attacks (+18.4 pp accuracy vs AT, with Injection alone at +30.4 pp accuracy / +40.6 pp F1), with a small regression on gradient attacks (-1.0 pp vs AT average) and a clean-accuracy cost (96.3% vs 98.3% Standard / 99.3% AT). Ablation: two-stage detection contributes +3.5 pp accuracy/+9.8 pp F1, attack-adaptive weighting +3.8 pp/+12.0 pp, attack-specific weights +3.7 pp/+10.9 pp -- each ablated variant recovers 1.8-3.2 pp clean accuracy, making the clean-vs-robust trade-off explicit. Cascade routing sends 76.5% of samples through fast Stage-1 screening with zero accuracy loss (84.9% overall, 96.3% clean, identical to non-cascaded), cutting Mahalanobis computations by 76.5% and attack-adaptive weighting invocations by 91%, though raw throughput drops to 0.81x (6,172->4,989 samples/sec) due to routing overhead. Cross-dataset on UNSW-NB15, AMDS shows essentially no improvement (-0.2 pp vs +4.2 pp on CIC), attributed to baseline accuracy collapsing to 52.9% (vs 80.7% on CIC) because UNSW's 190 features (vs 77) make an eps=0.02 budget ~1.57x effectively stronger under sqrt(d) scaling; weakening the budget to eps=0.01 partially recovers a positive effect (+0.5 pp accuracy, +1.9 pp F1). Under white-box adaptive attacks explicitly minimizing the ADS score, AMDS retains 94.4% accuracy with a 4.2% attack success rate (Improved Adaptive variant, vs 2.1% for Baseline Adaptive), and neither adaptive variant achieves any ADS-score reduction (0.0%).
core_contribution: AMDS (Attack-Aware Multi-Stage Defense System), a defense pipeline for ML-based network intrusion detection (NIDS) that combines four novel components on top of a 6-model ensemble (Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, Logistic Regression, MLP): (1) a three-signal Attack-Driven Suspicion Score (ADS) that linearly combines Shannon entropy, ensemble prediction-variance disagreement, and Mahalanobis anomaly distance via learned weights [alpha,beta,gamma]; (2) a two-stage adaptive detector that first runs generic weights and infers attack category (gradient vs. distribution-shift) from the anomaly score against threshold tau_anomaly=0.50, then re-scores with category-specific weights; (3) attack-adaptive model weighting, where per-model ensemble weights are set proportional to (category-accuracy)^3 and blended 50/50 with uniform weights when category-inference confidence is below 0.75; and (4) a 3-stage cascade router that resolves easy samples cheaply (confidence>0.85, disagreement<0.15) to avoid the O(d^2) Mahalanobis cost on most traffic. The system produces a dual output: binary adversarial-detection plus multiclass attack classification. This is distinct from the cited related work (ensemble adversarial training, TRADES, feature squeezing, diffusion-based purification, DynamicIDS routing), which the paper explicitly critiques for treating ensemble disagreement as noise, applying uniform (non-attack-specific) detection strategies, and providing only one of detection/classification rather than both -- these critiques frame the paper's own contribution, not results borrowed from those works.
threat_model: Machine-learning-based NIDS classifying network flow features (CSE-CIC-IDS2018: 77 features, 7 classes; UNSW-NB15: 190 features, 9 categories mapped to 5 classes) under test-time evasion. Seven adversarial attacks across two categories are used, generated with the Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART) v1.13 for gradient-based attacks and NumPy for distribution-shift attacks: gradient-based -- FGSM (eps=0.02, single-step L_inf), PGD-L_inf (eps=0.02, 20 steps, alpha=0.005), PGD-L2 (eps=0.02, 20 steps, L2), C&W-L2 (20 binary-search iterations, kappa=0, 500 samples), SPSA (100 queries, delta=0.01, black-box gradient estimation); distribution-shift -- Injection (per-feature Gaussian noise N(0,0.05*sigma_i)) and Morphing (systematic offset x_i' = x_i + 0.05*sigma_i). A separate white-box adaptive-adversary evaluation assumes full architectural knowledge of AMDS and explicitly optimizes to minimize the ADS score while preserving misclassification, using two variants: 'Baseline Adaptive' (random-direction search from benign samples) and 'Improved Adaptive' (FGSM-start with gradient approximation). The paper explicitly states this adaptive evaluation covers only two variants and 'does not constitute a formal robustness guarantee.'
limitations: Paper's own stated limitations (Section VI): (1) AMDS requires adversarial examples of the relevant attack families during weight learning, i.e. assumes partial prior knowledge of likely attack types; generalization to entirely unseen attack families is untested. (2) The binary category taxonomy (gradient vs. distribution-shift) may not capture real-world adversarial diversity, and the observed 100% category-inference accuracy should not be extrapolated to finer-grained taxonomies. (3) Learned weights are static at deployment; a persistent adversary could potentially craft evasion strategies over time, and only two adaptive-attack variants were tested (not a formal robustness guarantee). (4) Wide bootstrap confidence intervals on overall accuracy (95% CI: 74.1%-92.5%) reflect substantial heterogeneity across attack types, meaning summary statistics alone can be misleading. Additional limitations evident from the results themselves: AMDS underperforms AT on 4 of 5 gradient-based attacks (PGD-Linf, PGD-L2, C&W, and comparable on FGSM), the cross-dataset (UNSW-NB15) evaluation shows the method provides negligible benefit when baseline classifiers are already overwhelmed by attacks, and clean-data accuracy is consistently sacrificed (1.8-3.2 pp across ablations, up to 3.0 pp vs AT) as a trade-off for adversarial gains. The paper notes code/models are 'to be released upon acceptance' (not yet available at time of writing).

datasets_used: CSE-CIC-IDS2018 (primary; 77 features, 7 classes: Benign, Botnet, Brute-force, DDoS, DoS, Infiltration, Web attack)
UNSW-NB15 (cross-dataset generalization; 190 features, 9 attack categories mapped to 5 classes)
quantitative_results: Individual detection signal AUCs: 0.138-0.549 (weak in isolation)
Category-level weights: gradient (alpha=0.294, beta=0.452, gamma=0.253, AUC=0.702); distribution (alpha=0.443, beta=0.187, gamma=0.370, AUC=0.942)
Two-stage detection average AUC across 7 attacks: 0.942 vs generic-only 0.579 (+0.363) vs attack-specific-only 0.771 (+0.172)
Per-attack two-stage AUC: FGSM 0.943, PGD-Linf 0.943, PGD-L2 0.885, C&W 0.945, SPSA 0.943, Injection 0.966, Morphing 0.971
Category inference accuracy: 100% (7/7 attacks correctly categorized)
AMDS overall accuracy 84.8% vs Standard 80.9% (+3.9 pp) vs AT 80.3% (+4.5 pp)
AMDS overall F1 71.7% vs Standard 62.4% vs AT 62.7% (+9.0 pp vs AT)
Distribution-attack accuracy: AMDS 69.5% vs AT 51.2% (+18.4 pp); Injection AMDS 83.8% vs AT 53.4% (+30.4 pp accuracy, +40.6 pp F1)
Gradient-attack accuracy: AMDS 91.0% vs AT 92.0% (-1.0 pp)
Clean accuracy: AMDS 96.3% vs Standard 98.3% vs AT 99.3%
AT vs Standard ensemble: -0.6 pp accuracy, +0.3 pp F1 (AT shows minimal benefit)

Cascade router: Stage 1 resolves 76.5% of samples, Stage 2 23.4%, Stage 3 9.0%; accuracy identical cascaded vs full (84.9% overall / 96.3% clean); throughput 6,172 -> 4,989 samples/sec (0.81x); Mahalanobis calcs -76.5%, attack-adaptive weighting invocations -91%

Ablation: two-stage detection +3.5 pp accuracy / +9.8 pp F1; attack-adaptive weighting +3.8 pp / +12.0 pp; attack-specific weights +3.7 pp / +10.9 pp; ablated configs recover 1.8-3.2 pp clean accuracy

UNSW-NB15: baseline accuracy 52.9% vs CIC 80.7%; AMDS delta +4.2 pp (CIC) vs -0.2 pp (UNSW); at eps=0.01 on UNSW, AMDS delta recovers to +0.5 pp accuracy / +1.9 pp F1; effective attack strength scaling ~1.57x (sqrt(190/77))
Adaptive white-box attacks: AMDS maintains 94.4% accuracy, 4.2% attack success rate (Improved Adaptive) vs 2.1% (Baseline Adaptive); ADS-score reduction achieved by adversary: 0.0% in both variants
Overall accuracy 95% bootstrap CI: 74.09%-92.46% (point estimate 85.01%); F1-macro CI 60.86%-83.06% (73.08%); clean accuracy CI 95.32%-97.00% (96.25%)
baselines_compared: Adversarial Training (AT) Ensemble (PGD training, eps=0.02, 10 steps, 3 seeds)
Uniform ADS (AMDS with generic weights only, no category refinement)
